title: Salary Increase Calculator
name: Salary Increase Calculator
titleTag: Salary Increase Calculator | Smart Calculator Pro
metaDescription: Estimate a salary increase from either a raise amount or raise percentage. Review current salary, raise size, and new salary in one planning tool.
intro: Estimate how a raise changes salary using either a fixed raise amount or a raise percentage workflow.
shortDescription: Estimate raise amount, raise percentage, and new salary using either amount-based or percentage-based inputs.
longDescription: This salary increase calculator helps you estimate how a raise changes compensation under two common planning workflows. You can start with a known raise amount, such as a $4,000 annual increase, or start with a raise percentage, such as 5%. The tool then shows the implied raise amount, raise percentage, and new salary so you can compare compensation scenarios more clearly.
problemSolved: Helps compare current salary, raise size, and updated compensation in a clean planning workflow.

inputs.label: Current Salary
inputs.explanation: Enter the current annual salary before the raise is applied.

inputs.label: Raise Workflow
inputs.explanation: Choose whether you are starting with a raise amount or a raise percentage.

inputs.label: Raise Value
inputs.explanation: Enter the raise amount if you chose amount, or the raise percent if you chose percentage.

outputs.label: Current Salary
outputs.explanation: The starting annual salary used in the scenario.

outputs.label: Increase Amount
outputs.explanation: Estimated dollar value of the raise.

outputs.label: Increase Percent
outputs.explanation: Estimated raise size as a percentage of the current salary.

outputs.label: New Salary
outputs.explanation: Estimated annual salary after the raise is applied.

formula.label: Salary Increase Formulas
formula.expression: New Salary = Current Salary + Raise Amount; Raise Amount = Current Salary x Raise Percent
formula.plainText: If you start with a raise amount, the new salary is current salary plus that amount. If you start with a raise percentage, the calculator converts the percentage into a dollar increase first and then adds it to the current salary.

formulaExplanation: When you choose the raise amount workflow, the calculator adds the raise amount directly to the current salary.
When you choose the raise percentage workflow, the calculator multiplies current salary by the raise percentage to estimate the raise amount.
The tool always shows the raise in both dollars and percentage terms so the scenario is easy to compare.
This is a gross salary planning calculator. It does not estimate taxes, deductions, or changes in take-home pay by itself.
methodologyNotes: The calculator assumes the raise applies to the current annual salary amount you enter.
It is designed for planning and comparison, not payroll forecasting or contract interpretation.
Bonuses, commissions, overtime, benefits, and tax changes are not included in the output.
If you want to estimate take-home impact after the raise, pair the new salary with a gross to net or net to gross salary tool.

example.scenario: An employee earns $72,000 per year and wants to estimate the impact of a 6% raise.
example.resultSummary: The calculator estimates a raise amount of about $4,320, an increase percentage of 6%, and a new salary of about $76,320.

resultInterpretation.defaultText: Use the new salary as a gross compensation estimate first. If the raise decision affects budgeting, compare the updated salary with take-home pay or overtime alternatives before treating it as the full financial impact.

thresholds.label: Increase Percent
thresholds.interpretation: A raise in this range can materially change annual pay, but the real impact depends on taxes, deduction changes, and whether overtime or bonuses also shift.

warningMessages: A raise that looks meaningful in gross salary may feel smaller in practice once taxes, benefit costs, and changed deductions are considered.

faq.question: How do I calculate a salary increase from a percentage?
faq.answer: Multiply the current salary by the raise percentage to estimate the raise amount, then add that amount to the current salary.

faq.question: How do I calculate a raise percentage from a raise amount?
faq.answer: Divide the raise amount by the current salary and convert the result into a percentage. This calculator handles both directions depending on the workflow you choose.

faq.question: Does this calculator show the real take-home impact of a raise?
faq.answer: No. This tool shows gross salary changes only. If you want a rough take-home estimate after the raise, use the new salary with a gross to net salary calculator.

faq.question: Should I compare a raise with overtime pay?
faq.answer: Sometimes yes. If your work income can change through overtime as well as salary increases, compare both scenarios so you can see which path changes compensation more realistically.

faq.question: Can I use this for offer negotiations?
faq.answer: Yes. It is useful for raise discussions, compensation planning, and comparing what different increase scenarios would mean before you negotiate.

disclaimer: This salary increase calculator provides estimate-based gross salary planning only. It does not account for taxes, bonus structures, benefits, payroll timing, contract terms, or employer-specific compensation rules.
assumptions: Uses a simplified gross salary increase model based on current annual salary plus either a raise amount or raise percentage. It estimates salary only and does not include taxes, overtime, bonuses, or payroll deductions.
